assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT true of Meret Oppenheim's *Object*?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e8839bc0a9774d12b25fd10d270ba324.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Acclaim for the fur-covered Object had a negative effect on Oppenheim’s early career. For Oppenheim, the prestige and focus on this one object proved too much, and she spent more than a decade out of the artistic limelight, destroying much of the work she produced during that period.**

**[Read this essay to review](/a/meret-oppenheim-object-fur-covered-cup-saucer-and-spoon)**

choices.content: It was created at a moment when sculpted objects and assemblages had become prominent features of Surrealist art.
It insists that we imagine what sipping warm tea from this cup feels like, how the bristles would feel upon our lips.
Oppenheim supposedly created this work of art after a meeting in a cafe with Pablo Picasso and Dora Maar.
The work of art had a positive effect on the artist's early career.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Salvador Dalí described some of the Surrealist objects as “objects with a literal function.” These items were meant to be used, despite their unusual appearance.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e8839bc0a9774d12b25fd10d270ba324.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Salvador Dalí described some of the Surrealist objects as “objects with a symbolic function.” An ordinary, functional object was modified to create a new object that was both personal and poetic.** 

**[Read this essay to review](/a/meret-oppenheim-object-fur-covered-cup-saucer-and-spoon)**

**Meret Oppenheim's *Object* recalls the Surrealists' love of alchemical transformation by turning cool, smooth ceramic and metal into something warm and bristley.**

**[Read this essay to review](/a/meret-oppenheim-object-fur-covered-cup-saucer-and-spoon)**

choices.content: the Surrealists' interest in propriety and order.
the Surrealists' love of alchemical transformation.
